title: What is the Mission of the Church, Part 2
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
series: What is the Mission of the Church
source_files: What is the Mission of the Church 2 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt
outline: Review of Part 1: the question of church mission
Key New Testament passages on the church's calling
Mission as making disciples of all nations
Conclusion: The church's primary calling from Scripture
summary: Elder Nick Anderson continues the Sunday School series on the mission of the church, examining what the Bible says about the church's purpose. He works through key New Testament passages to establish a biblical definition of the church's mission.
transcript:
## Review of Chapter 1

Last week we established that the mission of the church is not everything we do in Jesus' name, nor is it everything we do in obedience to Christ. The mission, as we defined it, is the specific task we have been given to fulfill. We are going to get to that definition eventually. But first, we need to look at several passages that are sometimes cited as expressing the mission of the church — and we need to understand why each of them, on its own, does not adequately express that mission.

## Three Passages That Are Not the Great Commission

### Genesis 12:1–3

Christopher Wright, a writer on missiological questions, calls this one of the most important passages in a missiological reading of the Bible. Reggie McNeal, also a proponent of this passage, says it is a "simple but far-reaching covenant" in which God's people are charged with both the responsibility and the privilege to bless everyone. They make "go" and "be a blessing" the twin mission of the church.

There is nothing wrong with saying we ought to go and we ought to be a blessing. But making that the mission of the church introduces confusion. Let's look at the passage:

> *"Now the Lord said to Abram: 'Get out of your country, from your family and from your father's house, to a land that I will show you. I will make you a great nation; I will bless you and make your name great; and you shall be a blessing. I will bless those who bless you, and I will curse him who curses you; and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed.'"* (Genesis 12:1–3)

They make a point that "go" and "be a blessing" are both imperatives in the Hebrew — two commands. But when two imperatives are joined in Hebrew, the first is often the command and the second is the result of the first. That is exactly what we see in Genesis 42:18, where Joseph says "Do this and you will live" — both verbs are imperatives, but the meaning is: *if you do this, then this will happen*. The same pattern applies here in Genesis 12.

Even if the grammar pointed to two distinct commands, we would still need to ask: what does "be a blessing" mean? It is vague unless we define it from Scripture. And when Scripture interprets Scripture, Paul tells us in Galatians 3:8–9 what the blessing of Abraham means:

> *"The Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, preached the gospel to Abraham beforehand, saying, 'In you all the nations shall be blessed.' So then those who are of faith are blessed with believing Abraham."* (Galatians 3:8–9)

Being a blessing, according to Paul interpreting this very passage, is not merely feeding the poor or clothing the destitute. It means calling people to repentance and faith in Christ. And that is more specific than the broad, vague sense in which "be a blessing" is often used.

### Exodus 19:5–6

This is a somewhat unusual passage to cite as a Great Commission text. Christopher Wright and Reggie McNeal both use it. The argument runs: God calls his people to be a "kingdom of priests," and priests mediate God's presence; therefore our mission is to mediate God's presence to the world.

> *"Now therefore, if you will indeed obey my voice and keep my covenant, then you shall be a special treasure to me above all people; for all the earth is mine. And you shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation."* (Exodus 19:5–6)

But this does not actually teach what they claim, for at least five reasons. First, the Levitical priesthood was a mediatorial role to *placate* God's anger toward sinners, not to incarnate his presence. Second, the term "kingdom of priests" is better understood as a holy people, not as people incarnating God's presence. Third, if God were giving Israel a missionary mandate here, we would expect explicit instructions for carrying it out somewhere in the Mosaic law — but no such instructions exist. Fourth, Israel conquered the surrounding nations by military force, which is the opposite of "being a blessing" to them. And fifth, when the prophets condemn Israel throughout the Old Testament, they do not charge them with failing to fulfill a missionary mandate to the nations. If this were Israel's mission, we would expect to see it.

### Luke 4:16–21

This is probably the most commonly cited passage for the claim that the church's mission is social transformation. The Spirit of the Lord is upon Jesus to preach good news to the poor, to proclaim liberty to captives and recovery of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord.

The popular explanation says that Jesus came as a social liberator — his ministry was especially for the poor, and therefore we should be especially focused on the poor and social transformation. There is a kernel of truth here: Christians *should* influence society and help the downtrodden. But this reading misses what the text is actually saying.

Notice that the word "proclaim" appears multiple times: proclaim good news, proclaim liberty, proclaim the acceptable year. The one exception is "set at liberty the oppressed." The dominant action is *proclamation* — announcing, declaring, preaching. If this text sets the tone for the church's mission, it is primarily a mission of preaching.

Also, "the poor" here does not necessarily mean people without money. In the Isaiah context from which this is drawn, it can mean the broken-hearted, the afflicted, those who mourn, the meek. Throughout the New Testament this word can be taken literally or figuratively, as in spiritually poor. David Bosch puts it well: "The material rich can be spiritually poor; the material poor can also be spiritually poor." The poor are better understood as those who recognize their need for a Savior.

## The Great Commission Passages

Why do we focus specifically on the so-called Great Commission passages rather than the whole Bible? Several reasons. First, even if the Bible is essentially a book about God's mission, we still need to know: what is *our* job? Not everything God does is something we are supposed to do — Christ died for sinners, but we are not called to do that. Second, it makes sense to look to the New Testament for a theology of missions; the Old Testament is primarily concerned with the nation of Israel, and only in the New Testament — particularly Acts — do we find a model for mission work. Third, we should look to Jesus for our missiological directive. When Jesus was baptized, God said: "This is my beloved Son; hear him." Fourth, the placement of the Great Commission at the very end of the Gospels signals its strategic importance. These were among the last words Jesus spoke to his disciples, and last words carry weight.

### Matthew 28:16–20

> *"All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I have commanded you; and lo, I am with you always, even to the end of the age."* (Matthew 28:18–20)

Note the setting: the disciples were brought to a mountain in Galilee. Important things happen on mountains in Scripture — the giving of the law, the Transfiguration, the Sermon on the Mount. The mountaintop setting signals significance. Jesus first establishes his universal authority, then gives the commission, then promises his abiding presence to the very end of the age.

### Mark 16:15

> *"Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature."* (Mark 16:15)

And the result of obedience, according to verse 20: "they went out and preached everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word through the accompanying signs."

### Luke 24:44–49

> *"Repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name to all nations, beginning at Jerusalem. And you are witnesses of these things."* (Luke 24:47–48)

A clear command: be witnesses, proclaim repentance and forgiveness of sins in all nations.

### Acts 1:8

> *"But you shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be witnesses to me in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth."* (Acts 1:8)

These are the last words Jesus spoke before his ascension. Then he disappeared into the clouds and the disciples stood there staring. They had to be told, "You can do other things now." Those last words define their calling.

When we look at the book of Acts in light of this commission, the church's mission was not a society renewal program or care for the planet or material blessing of the poor. The gospel was preached immediately. A church was formed, devoted to the apostles' teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer. In Acts 6, when the apostles were overwhelmed with ministry needs, they appointed deacons to handle the physical care of the church — *so that they could remain focused on preaching and prayer*. The word spread. Churches were planted. Elders were appointed. The book ends exactly as it began, with preaching: Paul preaching boldly in Rome, the ends of the earth. The commission was being fulfilled.

### John 20:21

> *"As the Father has sent me, I also send you."* (John 20:21)

This is the shortest and perhaps most controversial commission passage. The question is: in what way is Jesus' mission a model for ours? Some argue it means we are to incarnate his presence — continuing his ministry of service. But to make serving the absolute number one priority is actually misleading. Jesus was not always with the poor and sick; sometimes he was alone in prayer, sometimes with wealthy people, sometimes being served himself. His mission was to save sinners from their sins. The healings and miracles were attestations of his identity and authority, not the mission itself. And we are not reincarnate copies of Jesus. We are representatives of him, called to bear witness to who he is and what he has done.

## The Mission Defined

With all of that covered, we can now define the mission of the church by answering seven questions:

**Who?** The disciples who received the Great Commission, and all who follow in their steps.

**Why?** Because Christ commanded us.

**What?** Preaching, teaching, proclaiming good news, testifying, making disciples, bearing witness to Jesus.

**Where?** All over the world — or to bring it closer to home: wherever you are, wherever you go. The command is not "get people to come here"; it is "go and tell."

**How?** By the power of the Holy Spirit working in us.

**When?** Until Jesus returns — right now, until the end of the age.

**To whom?** Everyone, indiscriminately. We do not know who will respond. We do not know when God is going to work in a particular heart. But we go and proclaim to every nation, whether we see fruit or not. There are missionaries who have labored in a country for twenty years without seeing a single convert. But all those people still need to hear the gospel.

Look at Paul's commission in Acts 26:16–18:

> *"For I have appeared to you for this purpose, to make you a minister and a witness both of the things which you have seen and of the things which I will yet reveal to you. I will deliver you from the Jewish people, as well as from the Gentiles, to whom I now send you, to open their eyes, in order to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who are sanctified by faith in me."* (Acts 26:16–18)

Paul planted churches, and those churches continued the same work. The Thessalonians were sounding forth the word in their region. The Philippians were expected to proclaim the gospel. In Ephesians 6, the armor of God passage — the shoes of readiness to proclaim the gospel — is addressed not to pastors but to all the saints. Not everyone will go to a foreign country. But you are a missionary in your neighborhood, at work, at school. Everywhere you go, you are to proclaim the gospel to the people around you.

**The mission of the church is to go into all the world and make disciples by declaring the gospel of Jesus Christ in the power of the Spirit, and gathering those disciples into churches that they might worship the Lord and obey his commands, now and in eternity, to the glory of God the Father.**

Or more simply: the mission of the church is to preach the gospel and make disciples.
